title: White House app auto-downloads to government phones, can't be uninstalled
gist: The White House app, developed by an Ohio company called 45Press under government contracts worth up to $8 million, was automatically pushed to the work phones of millions of federal employees and cannot be uninstalled. The app includes propaganda content curated from partisan sources, and cybersecurity investigations found it initially shared users' IP addresses with third parties and incorporated widgets from a Russia-based company called Elfsight that exposed the personal information of White House officials. State Department employees with foreign contacts on WhatsApp on the same phone are particularly concerned.

themes.theme: Government App Security and Mandatory Deployment Risk

insights.claim: A White House app developed by Ohio-based company 45Press—which received $1.5 million with eligibility for over $8 million in government contracts—was automatically pushed to the work phones of millions of federal employees across USDA, State Department, and Department of Labor, and cannot be uninstalled.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Mandatory government app deployment without employee consent or robust security vetting creates a systemic insider-threat and surveillance vector across federal device fleets.
insights.quote: Federal workers hate it and can't get rid of it

insights.claim: Cybersecurity experts found the White House app initially shared users' location data and IP addresses with third parties after its April launch; separately, an investigation found the app incorporated widgets from Russia-based Elfsight that exposed personal information of White House officials.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Foreign-vendor-linked code embedded in a mandatory federal government app represents an espionage-grade vulnerability that should alarm national security agencies.
insights.quote: the app incorporated widgets created by a Russia-based company called Elfsight, which exposed the personal information of White House officials

insights.claim: State Department employees with foreign diplomatic contacts on WhatsApp on the same government phone as the White House app expressed concern about the app's previously documented security vulnerabilities compromising those communications.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Mandatory installation on phones used for sensitive foreign-contact communications elevates the risk from personal privacy to diplomatic intelligence exposure.
insights.quote: I'm worried about the White House app vulnerabilities... many of my foreign contacts use WhatsApp, so we use that to communicate

insights.claim: The White House app's news section curates content exclusively from Fox, Breitbart, Reuters, the New York Post and other sources framed positively toward the administration, with a button that autofills a text to 'text President Trump' reading 'Greatest President Ever.'
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: The app functions as a mandatory propaganda distribution tool on government devices, raising First Amendment and Hatch Act concerns regarding political content deployment to federal workers.
insights.quote: It's shooting pure unadulterated propaganda into our veins

insights.claim: The White House app's privacy policy links only to the general WhiteHouse.gov privacy page, which contains no specific data policies for the mobile app beyond an email address—leaving users unable to determine how their data is collected or used.
insights.speaker: WIRED
insights.so_what: Absence of a meaningful app-specific privacy policy on a mandatory government tool creates legal risk and limits employees' ability to exercise any data rights.
insights.quote: there is a section for the policies for the White House Mobile App that includes only an email address and no details on the privacy or data policies for the app itself
